description:
You are a network engineer who has just run two quick diagnostic utilities that produced separate, very small text files in the directory /home/user/net_logs.  
  • /home/user/net_logs/ip_list.txt – one line per host, containing the host’s IP address followed by a colon and the destination port number.  
  • /home/user/net_logs/ping_ms.txt – one line per host, containing only the round-trip time in milliseconds (integer, no units, no extra spaces).

Your goal is to create a single, tab-separated summary file named /home/user/net_logs/summary.tsv that combines the two source files column-wise in the exact format below. Because you are writing a quick HOW-TO for a junior colleague, do this using one single terminal command that performs the column join (i.e. use either the classic *cut* family utilities such as paste, or an equivalent POSIX tool capable of the same single-shot column merge).

Required output file:  
File path: /home/user/net_logs/summary.tsv  
Required content (⇥ represents one literal tab, ⏎ is the mandatory Unix newline character):  

ip_port⇥ping_ms⏎  
192.168.0.1:80⇥32⏎  
192.168.0.2:443⇥85⏎  
10.0.0.1:22⇥12⏎  

Important verification rules the automated grader will apply:  
1 ) The file must be located exactly at /home/user/net_logs/summary.tsv.  
2 ) It must contain **precisely** 4 lines (one header plus three data lines).  
3 ) Each line must end with a single Unix newline (LF, “\n”).  
4 ) A single **tab** character must separate the two columns on every line. No extra spaces are allowed before or after the tab.  
5 ) The header must spell “ip_port” and “ping_ms” in lowercase exactly as shown.  
6 ) The three data lines must appear in the same order as the lines in the original ip_list.txt file (line-for-line correspondence with ping_ms.txt).  

Use whatever single command you prefer (e.g. *paste*) but do not manually edit the file with an editor—demonstrate how a one-liner solves this common column-joining need. After running the command, you may cat the resulting file so the colleague can see the output, but the presence and exact content of /home/user/net_logs/summary.tsv is what the automated test will grade.
truth:
Initial files that already exist when the task begins:

1) /home/user/net_logs/ip_list.txt  
Contents (each line ends with “\n”):  
192.168.0.1:80  
192.168.0.2:443  
10.0.0.1:22  

2) /home/user/net_logs/ping_ms.txt  
Contents (each line ends with “\n”):  
32  
85  
12  

Target file that must exist after the agent finishes:

/home/user/net_logs/summary.tsv  
Exact byte-for-byte content (⇥ = TAB, \n = LF):  
ip_port⇥ping_ms\n  
192.168.0.1:80⇥32\n  
192.168.0.2:443⇥85\n  
10.0.0.1:22⇥12\n  

No other files or directories are required to be modified or created. The agent will not have root privileges; normal user permissions on /home/user/net_logs permit creating and writing to summary.tsv.
